title: Coleslaw
subtitle: Creamy shredded cabbage and carrot in a tangy mayo dressing
excerpt: Finely shredded white cabbage and grated carrot dressed in a mixture of mayonnaise, cider vinegar, and a little mustard and celery salt. Rested for at least an hour before serving so the cabbage softens slightly and the dressing thickens. The American diner standard, made at home and better for it.
sourceNotes: Coleslaw has Dutch origins (koolsla, meaning cabbage salad) and was brought to North America by Dutch settlers. The mayonnaise-dressed version became the American standard in the twentieth century following the commercialisation of mayonnaise. It appears widely in American domestic cooking collections from the mid-twentieth century and is documented as a standard accompaniment to barbecue and sandwiches across the American South and Midwest.

content.text: Salt the cabbage before dressing it. Tossing with a little salt and leaving it for 20 minutes draws out excess moisture, this prevents the coleslaw from becoming watery as it sits. Squeeze out the released liquid, pat the cabbage dry, and then dress it. The result holds better, stays creamy for longer, and has a better texture.

The dressing needs balance: vinegar to cut the mayo, mustard for depth, sugar to take the edge off. Taste it before it goes on the cabbage. A dressing that seems a little sharp on its own will be just right after an hour's rest on the cabbage.

Toss the shredded {{cabbage-white}} of cabbage with 1 tsp of salt in a large bowl. Leave for 20 minutes, the cabbage will wilt slightly and a pool of liquid will form. Squeeze the cabbage firmly in handfuls to expel the liquid, then pat dry with kitchen paper.

Whisk together the {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, {{celery-salt}} of celery salt, and black pepper. Taste and adjust. It should be tangy and creamy in balance. Combine the squeezed cabbage and {{carrot}} of grated carrot in the bowl. Pour the dressing over and toss thoroughly until evenly coated. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving.

items.fix: Salt, rest, and squeeze the cabbage before adding the dressing, this step removes the water that would otherwise dilute the dressing over time
items.cause: Cabbage not salted and squeezed before dressing

items.fix: Add another teaspoon of cider vinegar and whisk; the correct consistency should coat the cabbage lightly and flow into all the gaps
items.cause: Too little vinegar for the amount of mayo
items.symptom: Dressing is too thick and clumps

items.fix: The dressing should taste assertively seasoned before it goes on the cabbage, add more salt, vinegar, or celery salt to correct
items.cause: Under-seasoned dressing; the cabbage dilutes seasoning as it rests

items.fix: Rest for a further hour; firm winter white cabbage takes longer to soften than summer varieties
items.cause: Not salted long enough, or very tightly packed (firm winter cabbage)
items.symptom: Cabbage is still very crunchy after 1 hour

content.text: Coleslaw takes its name from the Dutch koolsla, meaning cabbage salad. Dutch settlers brought it to North America in the seventeenth century. For most of the next two hundred years it was dressed with a warm vinegar-and-egg sauce, not mayo. The mayo version took over in the early twentieth century once jarred mayonnaise became easy to buy. By the mid-twentieth century, creamy coleslaw was the go-to side for American barbecue and diner sandwiches. The homemade version takes almost no effort and beats the shop-bought kind.
